0000.summary: This preface introduces UPES University of Tomorrow's Policy & Governance Manual as a unified framework for university-level governance with Addendum A covering faculty and staff-specific policies.

0001.title: Purpose of the Manual
0001.text:
# Purpose of the Manual

The UPES Policy & Governance Manual establishes the institutional framework governing the formulation, approval, implementation, and oversight of all University-level policies at UPES.

The Manual:

- Ensures consistency and transparency in governance
- Clearly distinguishes policies from operational instruments
- Serves as the single authoritative reference for academic and administrative decision-making
- Aligns institutional practices with applicable regulatory and statutory requirements

This Manual shall be the **primary and controlling reference** for all University policies.
0001.summary: It provides the framework for policy formulation, approval, implementation, and oversight, serving as the primary authoritative reference for all University decisions.

0002.title: Policy Hierarchy at UPES
0002.text:
# Policy Hierarchy at UPES

UPES follows a defined policy hierarchy to maintain governance clarity. Instruments at lower levels exist only to operationalise approved policies and shall not override higher-level instruments.
0002.summary: UPES maintains a governance hierarchy where lower-level instruments cannot override higher-level instruments and only operationalize approved policies.

0003.title: Order of Authority:
0003.text:
# Order of Authority:

1. University Policies: Approved institutional rules and governance provisions
2. Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs): Documents operationalising policy provisions
3. Guidelines / Frameworks: Advisory or interpretative references
4. Forms / Templates / Checklists: Transactional execution tools
5. Student FAQs: A separate documents to help explain University policies in simple language.

In case of inconsistency, University Policies shall prevail.
0003.summary: The hierarchy of authority is: University Policies, then SOPs, then Guidelines/Frameworks, then Forms/Templates, then Student FAQs - with University Policies prevailing over all others.

0004.title: Authority & Interpretation
0004.text:
# Authority & Interpretation

Policy formulation, approval, and oversight shall be exercised through the designated competent authority, including the exercise of discretionary powers where required.

Policies shall be interpreted in line with their stated purpose, scope, and applicable regulatory provisions. In the event of conflicting interpretations or conflicts between policies and subordinate instruments, the decision of the competent authority shall be final and binding.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0004.summary: The competent authority holds final decision-making power for policy formulation, approval, oversight, and resolving interpretation conflicts.

0005.title: Policy Domains
0005.text:
# Policy Domains

All University-level policies at UPES are classified under defined domains to ensure consistency and ease of reference. Each policy is assigned a single primary domain, without restricting cross-referencing where required.
0005.summary: All University-level policies at UPES must be classified under defined domains, with each policy assigned a single primary domain while allowing cross-referencing where required.

0008.title: ACADEMIC PROGRESSION FRAMEWORK
0008.text:
# ACADEMIC PROGRESSION FRAMEWORK

(Policy Code: AG-01)

This Policy shall come into force with immediate effect.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0008.summary: The Academic Progression Framework (Policy AG-01) takes effect immediately in January 2026 for UPES University of Technology.

0009.title: 1. Purpose
0009.text:
## 1. Purpose

The UPES Academic Progression Framework establishes the institutional architecture for academic progression from admission to graduation across all Schools and Programmes.

1.1 The Framework aims to:

1.1.1 Provide a unified, recovery-first progression logic across the University

1.1.2 Ensure consistency, fairness, transparency, and predictability in academic decisions

1.1.3 Anchor all standalone academic policies within a single institutional framework

1.2 This Framework shall be read prior to and in conjunction with all standalone academic policies governing assessment, attendance, progression, academic recovery, substitution, temporary exit, and graduation.
0009.summary: This Policy enables full-time undergraduates to pursue Minor, Double Major, or Independent Study, with statutory council programmes requiring extended duration and compliance overrides.

0010.title: 2. Guiding Principles of Academic Progression
0010.text:
## 2. Guiding Principles of Academic Progression

Academic progression at UPES is governed by the following non-negotiable principles:

2.1 Progression with Accountability

Students may progress semester-wise, provided academic deficiencies are addressed within defined structures and timelines.

2.2 Recovery-First Philosophy

Clearing failed courses takes precedence over unrestricted forward progression.

2.3 Academic Rigour

Flexibility shall not dilute academic standards, learning outcomes, or degree integrity.

2.4 Student Choice with Responsibility

Academic choice is accompanied by clear responsibility for outcomes and implications.

2.5 Transparency & Predictability

Progression rules, thresholds, and consequences are clearly articulated and uniformly applied.

2.6 Completion Within Prescribed Duration

All progression and recovery pathways operate strictly within UGC prescribed maximum Programme duration norms (N+2+1).
0010.summary: Students progress semester-wise only after clearing failed courses; rules are transparent with defined thresholds, and completion must occur within N+2+1 maximum duration.

0011.title: 3. Concept of Academic Progression
0011.text:
## 3. Concept of Academic Progression

3.1 Academic progression at UPES is credit-based, outcome-oriented, and continuous, comprising:

3.1.1 Course-level completion

3.1.2 Semester-wise progression

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

3.1.3 Credit accumulation
3.1.4 Programme completion

3.2 UPES follows a Continuous Academic Progression System, under which:

3.2.1 Students normally progress semester-wise even with pending academic deficiencies.
3.2.2 Academic deficiencies do not automatically interrupt semester progression.
3.2.3 All deficiencies must be addressed through Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP).

3.3 Progression is subject to:

3.3.1 Eligibility and prerequisite requirements.
3.3.2 Clearance of deficiencies within the prescribed maximum Programme duration.

3.4 Progression does not require all courses to be cleared in the first attempt. However, all required credits must be cleared prior to graduation.
3.5 Such progression is subject to academic eligibility conditions, including attendance requirements, prerequisite fulfilment, and policy-defined limits, as outlined in subsequent sections.
0011.summary: UPES allows semester-wise progression with pending deficiencies, cleared via Academic Recovery Pathways before graduation, subject to attendance and prerequisite requirements.

0012.title: 4. Structure of the Academic Year
0012.text:
## 4. Structure of the Academic Year

4.1 The academic year consists of:

4.1.1 Regular Semesters (Odd and Even)
4.1.2 Optional Summer Semester, where notified

4.2 Each Programme prescribes a recommended credit load, with flexibility subject to:

4.2.1 Maximum permissible credit limits*
4.2.2 Academic feasibility
4.2.3 Policy-defined approvals

* Maximum permissible credit limit is 27 credits per semester. For BCI/PCI-regulated programmes, where the prescribed semester load exceeds 24 credits, an additional credit overload of up to 15% of the semester's credit requirement may be permitted.

4.3 The Summer Semester functions as an academic recovery, improvement and/or acceleration mechanism, governed by standalone policies.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

### 4.4 Minimum Annual Credit Requirement

Each degree Programme at UPES shall be structured to ensure a minimum academic engagement of 40 credits per academic year, aligned with the standard duration of the Programme.

4.4.1 The minimum total credit requirements for undergraduate degree Programmes are as follows:

|  Standard Duration of Programme | Minimum Total Credits  |
| --- | --- |
|  Three-Year Degree Programme | 120 credits  |
|  Four-Year Degree / Honours Programme | 160 credits  |
|  Five-Year Integrated Degree Programme | 200 credits  |

4.4.2 Accordingly, every School / Cluster / Department / Centre of the University shall ensure that the annual credit offering for each Programme is not less than 40 credits, unless otherwise permitted under an approved accelerated or flexible progression pathway.

4.4.3 Where a Programme requires additional credits beyond the minimum prescribed for its duration, such requirements must be:

- 4.4.3.1 Academically justified and documented
- 4.4.3.2 Approved by the Board of Studies (BoS)
- 4.4.3.3 Approved by the Academic Council
0012.summary: Academic year has regular semesters and optional summer term; max 27 credits per semester; undergrad programs require minimum 120-200 total credits across 3-5 years.

0013.title: 5. Teaching-Learning & Assessment Governance
0013.text:
## 5. Teaching-Learning & Assessment Governance

5.1 Academic progression is anchored in structured teaching-learning processes, including:

- 5.1.1 Defined Course Outcomes (COs)
- 5.1.2 Approved syllabi and course plans
- 5.1.3 Transparent assessment schemes
- 5.1.4 Faculty responsibility for delivery, mentoring, and academic guidance

5.2 Assessment serves both:

- 5.2.1 Formative purposes (learning and feedback), and
- Summative purposes (certification)

5.3 The assessment cycle comprises:

- 5.3.1 Continuous / Internal Assessment
- 5.3.2 Mid-Course Evaluation

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

5.3.3 End-Term Examination

5.4 Detailed procedures are governed by standalone policies and SOPs.

6. Semester-Wise Progression & Eligibility

6.1 A student shall normally progress from one semester to the next irrespective of SGPA or CGPA, subject to:

1. Compliance with attendance requirements
2. Fulfilment of course prerequisites
3. Programme-specific eligibility norms

6.2 Students who do not meet prerequisites for a course shall not be permitted to register for that course.

6.3 For Programmes governed by external regulatory bodies (e.g., PCI, BCI), council regulations shall prevail.

0013.summary: Teaching-learning governance mandates defined course outcomes, approved syllabi, transparent assessment schemes, and faculty mentoring, with assessment covering continuous, mid-course, and end-term evaluations.

0020.title: 8. Credit Requirements for Award of Degree
0020.text:
## 8. Credit Requirements for Award of Degree

8.1 The award of any undergraduate or postgraduate degree shall be strictly contingent upon:

8.1.1 Successful completion of prescribed minimum credits

8.1.2 Compliance with approved academic structure
8.1.3 Clearance of all academic deficiencies

8.1.4 Reflection of credits in University records and the Academic Bank of Credits (ABC), where applicable
8.2 No degree shall be conferred unless all required credits are earned within the stipulated maximum duration.
0020.summary: All degrees require minimum prescribed credits, academic structure compliance, deficiency clearance, and ABC recording, all earned within maximum duration.

0021.title: 9. Attendance, Prerequisites & Temporary Exit
0021.text:
## 9. Attendance, Prerequisites & Temporary Exit

9.1 Attendance is an academic eligibility requirement, not a disciplinary measure
9.2 Attendance-based failure results in academic ineligibility for the concerned course
9.3 Prerequisites exist to ensure academic readiness and may restrict progression where unmet.
9.4 The University permits Temporary Exit / Break of Study for personal, medical, or academic reasons.
9.5 Temporary Exit:

9.5.1 Is a pause, not recovery or reset
9.5.2 Is distinct from Academic Year Back
9.5.3 Is governed by a standalone policy
0021.summary: Attendance is an eligibility requirement where failure results in ineligibility; prerequisites restrict progression if unmet; Temporary Exit allows a pause for personal, medical, or academic reasons.

0022.title: 10. Enhanced & Accelerated Academic Progression (Fast-tracking a degree)
0022.text:
## 10. Enhanced & Accelerated Academic Progression (Fast-tracking a degree)

UPES recognises students capable of accelerated or enhanced progression. Such progression:

10.1 Represents positive advancement, not recovery
10.2 Is governed by academic feasibility, credit limits, and approvals
10.3 Does not dilute academic standards

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0022.summary: Students may accelerate degree completion through approved enhanced progression based on academic feasibility and credit limits, without diluting academic standards.

0023.title: 11. Institutional Philosophy on Academic Recovery
0023.text:
## 11. Institutional Philosophy on Academic Recovery

UPES adopts a recovery-first academic philosophy, under which:

11.1 Students are provided structured, time-bound recovery opportunities
11.2 Recovery mechanisms are more rigorous than regular academic progression
11.3 Escalation occurs only when recovery proves ineffective
0023.summary: UPES implements a recovery-first philosophy providing structured, time-bound recovery opportunities with escalation only when recovery proves ineffective.

0024.title: 12. Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP)
0024.text:
## 12. Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP)

Academic Recovery Pathways constitute the primary mechanism for addressing academic deficiencies and include:

12.1 Supplementary Examination
12.2 Summer Semester
12.3 Directed Reading (DR)
12.4 Regular Repeat
12.5 Course Substitution (where permitted)
12.6 Academic Year Back (Recovery & Progression Mode)

ARP options are not automatic entitlements and are governed by eligibility and approval under standalone policies.
0024.summary: ARP offers six recovery options—supplementary exams, summer semester, directed reading, repeats, substitutions, or year back—but requires eligibility approval and is not automatic.

0025.title: 13. Academic Year Back (Recovery & Progression Mode)
0025.text:
## 13. Academic Year Back (Recovery & Progression Mode)

Academic Year Back is a mandatory recovery-and-progression control mechanism applicable where academic difficulty becomes systemic. Applicable from admission year 2025.

13.1 Trigger

13.1.1 Failure of 50% or more credits in an academic year (Odd + Even semesters combined)

13.2 Key Positioning

13.2.1 Mandatory and non-discretionary
13.2.2 Does not constitute repetition of the entire academic year
13.2.3 Does not annul earned credits
13.2.4 Requires compulsory backlog clearance before further progression
13.2.5 Permits limited, conditional forward movement
13.2.6 Applicable from admission year 2025.

Detailed operational rules are governed by the Academic Year Back & Progression Policy.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0025.summary: Mandatory for students failing 50%+ credits, requires backlog clearance before progression; applies from admission year 2025; doesn't repeat year or annul earned credits.

0026.title: 14. Academic Records
0026.text:
## 14. Academic Records

UPES maintains academic records that are:

14.1 Accurate
14.2 Transparent

0027.title: 15. Equity, Due Process & Appeals
0027.text:
## 15. Equity, Due Process & Appeals

Academic progression rules are applied uniformly across Schools and Programmes through:

15.1 Defined eligibility criteria
15.2 Documented approval workflows
15.3 Formal communication and record entry

Appeals and grievances are addressed through established institutional mechanisms.
0027.summary: Academic progression applies uniformly across Schools via defined eligibility criteria, documented approval workflows, and formal record-keeping, with appeals handled through institutional mechanisms.

0030.title: ASSESSMENT & EVALUATION POLICY
0030.text:
# ASSESSMENT & EVALUATION POLICY

(Policy Code: AG-02)

This Policy shall come into force with immediate effect

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0030.summary: Assessment and Evaluation Policy (AG-02) at UPES University of Technology, effective January 2026 with immediate effect.

0031.title: 1. Purpose
0031.text:
## 1. Purpose

This Policy establishes a comprehensive, transparent, and academically rigorous framework for assessment and evaluation of student learning at UPES.

The objectives of this Policy are to:

1.1 Ensure fairness, consistency, and equity in assessment practices across all Schools and Programmes
1.2 Promote continuous learning through formative assessment while retaining appropriate summative evaluation
1.3 Uphold academic integrity and credibility of University awards
0031.summary: This Policy establishes a framework for fair, consistent assessment and evaluation at UPES to promote continuous learning and uphold academic integrity.

0032.title: 2. Scope & Applicability
0032.text:
## 2. Scope & Applicability

This Policy applies to:

2.1 All Undergraduate (UG) and Postgraduate (PG) Programmes offered by UPES
2.2 All courses delivered in regular academic semesters
2.3 All students, faculty members, and academic administrators involved in assessment and evaluation

This Policy shall be read in conjunction with:

2.4 Examination and Allied Provisions, Regulations (as notified by the Office of the Controller of Examinations)

Wherever operational details are not specified herein, the Examination and Allied Provisions, Regulations shall prevail.
0032.summary: Covers all UG/PG programmes, regular semester courses, and applies to students, faculty, and administrators; Examination and Allied Provisions prevail for unspecified matters.

0033.title: 3. Assessment Philosophy
0033.text:
## 3. Assessment Philosophy

UPES follows an instructor-led, outcome-oriented, and predominantly formative (continuous) evaluation system.

The assessment framework is designed to:

3.1 Evaluate learning progressively across a semester
3.2 Reduce over-dependence on a single final examination
3.3 Measure both conceptual understanding and applied competence
3.4 Provide timely academic feedback to students

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

Assessment and evaluation shall be aligned with Course Outcomes (COs), Programme Outcomes (POs), and approved curriculum structures.
0033.summary: UPES uses instructor-led formative assessment to progressively evaluate learning, reduce final exam dependence, measure conceptual and applied competence, and align with COs/POs.

0034.title: 4. Definitions
0034.text:
## 4. Definitions

|  Term | Definition  |
| --- | --- |
|  Assessment | The process of measuring student learning through structured academic tasks and evaluations.  |
|  Evaluation | The academic judgment of student performance based on assessed outcomes.  |
|  Continuous Assessment (CA) | All in-semester assessment components conducted before the End-Term Examination.  |
|  Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE) | A formal assessment conducted around the midpoint of the semester, forming part of CA.  |
|  End-Term Examination | The final assessment conducted at the end of the semester to evaluate cumulative learning outcomes.  |
|  Absolute Grading | A grading system in which student performance is evaluated against predefined and fixed performance thresholds, independent of peer performance. Grade boundaries and evaluation criteria are declared in advance and applied uniformly to all students enrolled in the course.  |
|  Relative Grading | A grading system in which student performance is evaluated in relation to the performance of the cohort, and grades are assigned based on comparative academic standing rather than fixed score thresholds. Grade boundaries are determined through approved academic judgment and are not disclosed to students.  |
0034.summary: This section defines assessment terminology including Continuous Assessment, Mid-Course Evaluation, End-Term Examinations, and two grading systems: Absolute Grading with pre-declared fixed thresholds versus Relative Grading based on cohort comparison.

0035.title: 5. Standard Assessment Structure
0035.text:
## 5. Standard Assessment Structure

As a standard University practice, theory courses in all Undergraduate (UG) and Postgraduate (PG) Programmes shall follow the assessment structures outlined below, based on the year of enrolment

### 5.1 Enrolment Year 2023-24 Onwards

Theory courses shall be evaluated using the following assessment structure:

|  Assessment Component | Weightage  |
| --- | --- |
|  Continuous Assessment (CA) | 70%  |
|  - Internal Assessments | 50%  |
|  - Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE) | 20%  |

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

|  End-Term Examination | 30%  |
| --- | --- |
|  Total | 100%  |

For clarity, the Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE) is an integral component of Continuous Assessment (CA) and shall not be treated as a standalone assessment category.

* The assessment structure may vary across Schools, programmes, or specific courses. Any deviation from the provisions outlined above should follow specifications in Clause 6 below.

### 5.2 Enrolment Years 2021-22 and 2022-23

Students enrolled in the academic years 2021-22 and 2022-23 shall continue to be governed by the assessment structure applicable at the time of their enrolment, as detailed below:

|  Assessment Component | Weightage  |
| --- | --- |
|  Internal Assessment | 30%  |
|  Mid-Semester Examination | 20%  |
|  End-Term Examination | 50%  |
|  Total | 100%  |

This differential assessment structure ensures academic continuity for earlier cohorts while implementing the revised assessment framework for students enrolled from Academic Year 2023-24 onwards.
0035.summary: From 2023-24, theory courses use 70% Continuous Assessment (50% internal + 20% MCE) and 30% End-Term; prior cohorts follow 30/20/50 split.

0036.title: 6. Approved Deviations from Standard Assessment Structure
0036.text:
## 6. Approved Deviations from Standard Assessment Structure

### 6.1 Permissible Deviations

Recognising disciplinary diversity and pedagogical requirements, certain Schools/Programmes/Courses may adopt an alternative assessment structure.

Such deviation shall be permitted only when:

6.1.1 The academic nature of the course justifies continuous evaluation (e.g., studio-based, laboratory-intensive, design-oriented, practice-driven, project-centric, or skill-based courses)

6.1.2 The assessment design is:

6.1.2.1 Academically defensible
6.1.2.2 Outcome-aligned
6.1.2.3 Clearly documented

6.1.3 Prior approval of the Academic Council is obtained

Post-facto approvals, instructor-level decisions, or ad-hoc deviations are not permitted.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

### 6.2 Transparency to Students

For the approved deviations:

6.2.1 The assessment structure shall be:

6.2.1.1 Explicitly stated in the course syllabus
6.2.1.2 Communicated to students at the start of the semester
6.2.1.3 Uniform across all sections of the course

A 100% CA structure shall not be construed as reduced academic rigor.
0036.summary: Deviations require Academic Council pre-approval, must justify continuous evaluation need, be academically defensible, outcome-aligned, documented, and communicated to students upfront.

0037.title: 7. Continuous / Formative Assessment (CA)
0037.text:
## 7. Continuous / Formative Assessment (CA)

### 7.1 Purpose

Continuous Assessment evaluates student performance throughout the semester and is intended to:

7.1.1 Encourage sustained academic engagement
7.1.2 Provide formative feedback
7.1.3 Assess diverse competencies beyond written examinations

### 7.2 Components of CA

Depending on course design, CA may include one or more of the following components:

7.2.1 Assignments
7.2.2 Quizzes and class tests (including surprise quizzes)
7.2.3 Mid-Course Evaluations (MCE)
7.2.4 Projects and mini projects
7.2.5 Presentations and group discussions
7.2.6 Laboratory and practical assessments
7.2.7 Term papers
7.2.8 Open-book examinations
7.2.9 Class participation
7.2.10 Other academically approved tasks as defined in the course plan

### 7.3 Disclosure of CA Scheme

For each course, the instructor shall ensure that the following are communicated on or before the first day of class and published on the University's Learning Management System (LMS):

7.3.1 CA components

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

7.3.2 Number of assessments
7.3.3 Individual weightages
7.3.4 Mode of evaluation

No new CA component or weightage change shall be introduced mid-semester without formal academic approval of the respective Dean.
0037.summary: Instructors must disclose all CA components, weights, and evaluation methods on the first day via LMS; no changes allowed mid-semester without the Dean's formal approval.

0038.title: 8. Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE)
0038.text:
## 8. Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE)

8.1 MCE is a **mandatory and formal 2 hour assessment** conducted around the midpoint of the semester
8.2 It evaluates learning outcomes covered in the first half of the course
8.3 MCE carries **20% weightage** as part of Continuous Assessment
8.4 Marks obtained in MCE contribute directly to the final course grade
8.5 Exceptions as per early release policy may be allowed.
8.6 For non-theory courses, the assessment components and their respective weightages or evaluation modes may vary as prescribed by the concerned School, subject to prior approval by the Board of Study.
0038.summary: MCE is a mandatory 2-hour formal assessment at semester midpoint, worth 20% of continuous assessment and contributing directly to the final course grade.

0039.title: 9. End-Term / Summative Examination (ETE)
0039.text:
## 9. End-Term / Summative Examination (ETE)

9.1 The End-Term Examination is the **final summative assessment** conducted at the end of the semester
9.2 It evaluates cumulative course learning outcomes
9.3 ETE carries **30% weightage**, unless an approved deviation exists
9.4 Eligibility to appear for ETE is governed by:

9.4.1 Attendance norms
9.4.2 Submission of Teaching Quality Feedback
9.4.3 No fees and disciplinary hold

9.5 Appearance in the End-Term Examination is **mandatory** for successful completion of a course.
9.6 A student who does not appear for the End-Term Examination shall be **declared failed in the course**, irrespective of marks obtained in Continuous Assessment.
9.7 For non-theory courses, the assessment components and their respective weightages or evaluation modes may vary as prescribed by the concerned School, subject to prior approval by the Board of Study.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0039.summary: ETE is mandatory final exam worth 30% weightage; failure to appear results in course failure regardless of Continuous Assessment marks.

0040.title: 10. Consistency Across Sections & Moderation
0040.text:
## 10. Consistency Across Sections & Moderation

10.1 For courses offered across multiple sections or batches:

10.1.1 Assessment components and weightages shall be uniform
10.1.2 Evaluation standards shall be consistently applied

10.2 Final grades may be subject to academic moderation through approved University mechanisms to ensure parity and fairness
10.3 Moderation processes are internal and confidential
0040.summary: Courses across multiple sections must use uniform assessment components and weightages, with final grades subject to internal University moderation for fairness.

0041.title: 11. Final Grade Calculation
0041.text:
## 11. Final Grade Calculation

The final grade awarded in a course shall reflect the student's performance in:

11.1 Continuous Assessment (IA + Mid-Course Evaluation) - 70%, and
11.2 End-Term Examination - 30%, as per the approved assessment structure.
11.3 Course grades awarded under this Policy shall be used for the computation of Semester Grade Point Average (SGPA) and Cumulative Grade Point Average (CGPA), in accordance with the Academic Progression Framework.
0041.summary: Final grades combine continuous assessment (70%) and end-term exam (30%), used for SGPA/CGPA under Academic Progression Framework.

0042.title: 12. Grading System
0042.text:
## 12. Grading System

Depending on batch size and academic context, the University adopts either an Absolute Grading System or a Relative Grading System, as defined below.

The applicable grading system for a course shall be declared at the beginning of the semester and shall remain unchanged for the duration of the course.

### 12.1 Applicability of Absolute and Relative Grading

#### 12.1.1 Absolute Grading System

The Absolute Grading System shall be followed when the batch size is up to and including twenty-one (21) students.

Under this system:

12.1.1.1 Grades are awarded based on predefined performance thresholds
12.1.1.2 Student performance is evaluated independently of peer performance
12.1.1.3 Grade boundaries reflect absolute achievement standards
12.1.1.4 Grade cut-offs and grading rubrics are disclosed to students in advance

#### 12.1.2 Relative Grading System

The Relative Grading System shall be followed when the batch size exceeds twenty-one (21) students.

Under this system:

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

12.1.2.1 Grades are awarded based on relative performance within the cohort
12.1.2.2 Grade boundaries are determined using approved academic judgment
12.1.2.3 Grade distribution reflects cohort performance while maintaining academic rigor

### 12.2 Grade Boundaries and Transparency

#### 12.2.1 Relative Grading

In the case of Relative Grading:

12.2.1.1 Grade boundaries are determined based on cohort performance
12.2.1.2 Grade cut-offs remain confidential
12.2.1.3 Determination of grade boundaries is a prerogative of the course instructor, subject to academic oversight

Students shall be informed of:

12.2.1.4 The grading approach being followed (relative), and
12.2.1.5 The assessment components and their respective weightages

However, specific grade cut-offs shall not be disclosed under the relative grading scheme.

#### 12.2.2 Absolute Grading

In the case of Absolute Grading:

12.2.2.1 The course instructor shall:
12.2.2.1.1 Declare the assessment criteria and grading rubric
12.2.2.1.2 Announce the grade boundaries at the beginning of the semester
12.2.2.1.3 Publish the same as part of the course outline / syllabus

The University provides a structure for absolute grade boundaries for guidance (Academic Progression Framework, Section 7).

### 12.3 Consistency and Governance

12.3.1 The grading system (absolute or relative), once declared for a course:

12.3.1.1 Shall not be altered mid-semester
12.3.1.2 Shall apply uniformly to all students registered in the course

12.3.2 Any exception or deviation from the above provisions shall require prior approval through the University's Academic Council / Board of Study.
0042.summary: The university applies Absolute Grading for ≤21 students using predefined thresholds, or Relative Grading for >21 students based on cohort performance, fixed at semester start.

0043.title: 13. Passing Requirements
0043.text:
## 13. Passing Requirements

To successfully complete a course, a student must:

13.1 Meet the minimum course passing criteria, and

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

13.2 Fulfil any component-wise minimum requirements, where prescribed
Semester-level progression is governed by separate Academic Progression policies.

|  Category | Programme Level | Rule / Requirement  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  Failure Threshold | Undergraduate (UG) | A total score of less than 35 marks in a course shall result in an F (Fail) grade  |
|  Failure Threshold | Postgraduate (PG) | A total score of less than 40 marks in a course shall result in an F (Fail) grade  |
|  Minimum Passing Grade | UG & PG | Grade C is the minimum passing grade. Students awarded Grade C or above are deemed to have successfully completed the course  |
|  Highest Grade | UG & PG | Students scoring 85 marks or above shall be awarded the highest grade (Grade O)  |

13.3 Accumulation of course failures, SGPA/CGPA outcomes, and credit completion under this Policy may result in Academic Year Back (AYB) or other academic recovery statuses, strictly as governed by the Academic Year Back & Progression Policy of the University.

14. Student Rights: Viewing, Re-evaluation & Grievance

14.1 Viewing of Answer Scripts

14.1.1 Students may view evaluated End-Term answer scripts, where applicable, on dates notified in the academic calendar
14.1.2 Requests outside the notified window shall not be entertained

14.2 Re-evaluation

14.2.1 Re-evaluation is permitted only for End-Term Examination components
14.2.2 Continuous Assessment components are not eligible for re-evaluation
14.2.3 Procedures and timelines are notified by the Office of the Controller of Examinations (CoE)

14.3 Grievance Redressal

Assessment-related grievances shall be addressed through prescribed academic grievance redressal mechanisms as notified by the University. (Academic Grievance Redressal Policy)

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0043.summary: Undergraduate students fail below 35 marks, postgraduate below 40 marks; Grade C is minimum passing, Grade O for 85+ marks.

0044.title: 15. Supplementary Assessment
0044.text:
## 15. Supplementary Assessment

15.1 Where applicable, Supplementary Examinations shall carry **weightage equivalent to the End-Term Examination**

15.2 Continuous Assessment marks shall remain unchanged

15.3 There shall be **no grade capping** for Supplementary Examinations

15.4 Detailed rules governing Supplementary, Summer, and Directed Reading assessments are covered under separate policies and regulations
0044.summary: Supplementary Examinations carry equal weightage to End-Term Exams, with unchanged Continuous Assessment marks and no grade capping.

0045.title: 16. Academic Integrity
0045.text:
## 16. Academic Integrity

All assessment and evaluation activities are subject to University regulations on **academic integrity and examination conduct**.

Any form of malpractice, misconduct, or unfair means shall attract penalties as per applicable regulations, independent of this Policy.
0045.summary: Assessment activities are subject to university academic integrity regulations; malpractice, misconduct, or unfair means result in penalties per applicable rules.

0046.title: 17. Review & Amendments
0046.text:
## 17. Review & Amendments

This Policy shall be reviewed periodically to ensure continued relevance, regulatory compliance, and academic rigor.

Any amendment shall require approval through the University's Academic Council.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0046.summary: This Policy is reviewed periodically to ensure relevance, compliance, and academic rigor, and any amendment requires Academic Council approval.

0047.title: ATTENDANCE POLICY
0047.text:
# ATTENDANCE POLICY

(Policy Code: AG-03)

This Policy shall come into force with immediate effect.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

## 1. Purpose

1.1 The Attendance Policy establishes a clear, uniform, and non-negotiable framework governing student attendance at UPES.

1.2 It reinforces the University's commitment to academic discipline, classroom engagement, and learning continuity, while allowing flexibility for unforeseen circumstances.

## 2. Governing Principles

### 2.1 Attendance Expected: 100%

Students are expected to attend all scheduled academic activities for every registered course, including lectures, tutorials, laboratories, studios, fieldwork, and other instructional sessions.

### 2.2 Built-in Flexibility: 25%

A maximum attendance shortfall of up to 25% is inherently permitted to accommodate unforeseen, routine, or incidental circumstances during a semester.

### 2.3 Strict Eligibility Threshold: 75%

Maintenance of minimum 75% attendance per course is a mandatory eligibility condition to appear for the End-Term (Summative) Examination.

These principles are derived from the UPES Academic Handbook and applicable regulatory norms.

## 3. Scope of Application

This policy applies to:

3.1 All undergraduate and postgraduate students
3.2 All credit-bearing or non-credit bearing courses, including:
3.3 Theory
3.4 Laboratories / Studios
3.5 Project-linked courses

Fieldwork and experiential components

3.7 Audit Courses
3.8 All modes of delivery:
3.9 Regular Semester
3.10 Summer Semester
3.11 Directed Reading
3.12 Regular Course Repeat

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

## 4. Attendance Recording

4.1 Attendance shall be recorded through University-approved systems (ERP / biometric / LMS-integrated mechanisms, as applicable).
4.2 Attendance records maintained in the official system shall be treated as final and binding.

## 5. Permissible Attendance Shortfall (Up to 25%)

A shortfall of up to 25% is automatically permitted to account for:

5.1 Illness
5.2 Personal exigencies
5.3 Logistical or situational disruptions
5.4 Other unforeseen circumstances
5.5 This buffer:
5.6 Does not require approval
5.7 Does not require documentation

## 6. Minimum Attendance Requirement (Strict)

6.1 Students must maintain at least 75% attendance in each registered course
6.2 Attendance is calculated course-wise, not Programme-wise or semester-wise
6.3 No rounding up, approximation, or discretionary adjustment shall be permitted

Failure to meet this requirement results in academic debarment for the concerned course.

## 7. Debarment Due to Attendance Shortage

### 7.1 Conditions for Debarment

A student shall be debarred from appearing in the End-Term Examination of a course if attendance is below 75%, irrespective of performance in Continuous Assessment (CA) or Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE).

High CA/MCE scores, assignments, projects, or mid-semester performance shall not offset attendance shortage.

### 7.2 Academic Consequences

7.2.1 The student shall be awarded an 'Ab' grade in the concerned course due to attendance shortage

Such students:

7.2.2 Are not eligible for Supplementary Examination
7.2.3 Must repeat the entire course when next offered (Regular Repeat or Summer Semester or Directed Reading)

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

## 8. Student Responsibility

Students are solely responsible for:

- 8.1 Monitoring their attendance
- 8.2 Ensuring compliance with eligibility requirements

Under no circumstances shall attendance relaxation be granted.

## 9. Authority, Interpretation & University Discretion

The University, through its Academic Council or other competent authority, reserves the right to:

- 9.1 Interpret and clarify this policy
- 9.2 Modify attendance rules, thresholds, calculation mechanisms, or enforcement processes
- 9.3 Amend or update provisions to ensure alignment with regulatory requirements, academic standards, and institutional priorities

Any such changes shall be notified through official University communication channels and shall be binding from the date specified.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0047.summary: Students must maintain minimum 75% attendance per course to be eligible for End-Term Examinations, with built-in flexibility of up to 25% shortfall permitted.

0048.title: ACADEMIC RECOVERY PATHWAYS (ARP) POLICY
0048.text:
# ACADEMIC RECOVERY PATHWAYS (ARP) POLICY

(Policy Code: AG-04)

This Policy shall come into force with immediate effect.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0048.summary: This policy establishes Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP) under code AG-04, effective January 2026 at UPES University of Technology.

0049.title: 1. Purpose
0049.text:
## 1. Purpose

1.1 The Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP) Policy establishes a **structured, uniform, and rigorously governed framework** enabling students to recover from academic setbacks **without dilution of academic standards**, while ensuring:

1.1.1 Compliance with UGC regulations
1.1.2 Alignment with NEP 2020 and NCrF principles
1.1.3 Timely academic progression within prescribed maximum Programme duration norms

1.2 This Policy defines **limited, clearly governed recovery mechanisms** for addressing academic failure, attendance-related ineligibility, prerequisite blocks, and accumulated backlogs affecting academic progression.
0049.summary: Establishes ARP framework enabling students to recover from academic failures while maintaining UGC compliance and NEP 2020 alignment within maximum programme duration norms.

0050.title: 2. Academic Progression & Recovery Philosophy
0050.text:
## 2. Academic Progression & Recovery Philosophy

2.1 UPES follows a **Recovery-First Academic Progression Model**, wherein:

2.1.1 Clearing failed courses takes precedence over accumulation of new academic load
2.1.2 Progression is governed by **defined attempt limits**, not discretionary approvals
2.1.3 Academic flexibility is permitted **only within approved and capped recovery pathways**
2.1.4 Progression aligns with UGC-prescribed **maximum Programme duration norms (N + 2 + 1)**

### 2.2 Principle of Academic Rigour in Recovery

All Academic Recovery Pathways shall be **academically more rigorous than regular course attempts**.

Recovery pathways:

2.2.1 Require higher academic discipline, preparedness, and engagement
2.2.2 Are subject to stricter eligibility conditions and scrutiny
2.2.3 Shall not be construed as lenient, easier, or alternative pathways to bypass regular academic requirements

### 2.3 Principle of Enhanced Academic Accountability

Academic Recovery Pathways exist to **restore academic standing**, not to compensate for inadequate academic discipline.

Accordingly:

2.3.1 Eligibility for recovery is conditional and not automatic
2.3.2 Assessment standards shall be higher than regular attempts

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

2.3.3 Monitoring, enforcement, and compliance expectations shall be stricter
2.3.4 Recovery pathways shall not be used to offset repeated underperformance or attendance non-compliance
0050.summary: UPES prioritizes clearing failed courses before new enrollment, enforces defined attempt limits, permits recovery only through capped pathways, and requires higher assessment standards.

0051.title: 3. Scope
0051.text:
## 3. Scope

3.1 This Policy applies to:

3.1.1 All full-time Undergraduate and Postgraduate students of UPES
3.1.2 All Schools, Departments, and Academic Units
3.1.3 All credit-bearing courses, unless explicitly excluded

3.2 This Policy governs recovery from:

3.2.1 Academic failure (F grade)
3.2.2 Attendance-based failure (Debarment)
3.2.3 Prerequisite-based progression blocks
3.2.4 Accumulated backlogs affecting progression

3.3 For Programmes governed by statutory or professional regulatory bodies (including but not limited to the Bar Council of India (BCI) and the Pharmacy Council of India (PCI)), the applicable regulations of such bodies shall prevail in the event of any inconsistency with this Policy. Academic recovery options, eligibility, and attempt limits for such Programmes shall be implemented strictly in accordance with the relevant regulatory requirements.
0051.summary: Applies to all full-time UPES students, Schools, Departments, and credit courses; governs F grade, attendance debarment, prerequisite blocks, and backlog recovery, with statutory body regulations taking precedence.

0052.title: 4. Definitions
0052.text:
## 4. Definitions

|  Term | Definition  |
| --- | --- |
|  N + 2 + 1 (Maximum Programme Duration) | The maximum time permitted for completion of a Programme, calculated as the minimum prescribed duration of the Programme (N) plus two (2) additional academic years, and one (1) additional academic year with VC's approval.  |
|  Continuous Assessment (CA) | All internal evaluation components excluding End-Term Examination  |
|  Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE) | Mid-semester assessment conducted as part of CA  |
|  Backlog | A course with grade F, Ab, or I (I subsequently converted to F)  |
|  Debarred | Student ineligible for End-Term Examination due to attendance below 75%  |
|  Regular Attempt | First formal registration and assessment attempt of a course  |

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

|  Recovery Attempt | An approved attempt through ARP mechanisms  |
| --- | --- |
|  Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP) | Approved mechanisms for clearing failed courses  |
|  Prerequisite Block | Inability to register for a higher-level course due to uncleared prerequisite  |

|  ERP / Academic Portal | University system for registration, grading, tracking, and audit  |
0052.summary: This section defines academic terms including maximum programme duration (N+2+1 years), CA excludes End-Term, 75% attendance threshold for exam eligibility, and backlog as F/Ab/I grades.

0053.title: 5. Approved Academic Recovery Pathways
0053.text:
## 5. Approved Academic Recovery Pathways

5.1 UPES formally recognises the following six (6) Academic Recovery Pathways:

5.1.1 Supplementary Examination (Supplementary Examination Policy)
5.1.2 Summer Semester (Summer Semester Policy)
5.1.3 Directed Reading (DR) (Directed Reading (DR) Policy)
5.1.4 Regular Repeat (Full Repeat) (Regular Course Repeat Policy)
5.1.5 Substitution of Eligible Elective Courses (Course Substitution Policy)
5.1.6 Academic Year Back (Recovery & Progression Mode) (Academic Year Back & Progression Policy)

5.2 No recovery mechanism beyond those listed above shall be permitted.
5.3 All recovery attempts undertaken by a student shall be recorded and reflected in the official grade card and ERP.
0053.summary: UPES formally recognises six Academic Recovery Pathways—Supplementary Exam, Summer Semester, Directed Reading, Repeat, Substitution, and Year Back—all recorded in official records.

0054.title: 6. Course Attempt Structure & Limits
0054.text:
## 6. Course Attempt Structure & Limits

### 6.1 Attempt Structure

Each course shall have:

6.1.1 One (1) Regular Attempt, and
6.1.2 A maximum of two (2) Recovery Attempts through approved Academic Recovery Pathways.

6.2 Recovery attempts may be undertaken through:

6.2.1 Supplementary Examination
6.2.2 Summer Semester
6.2.3 Directed Reading (DR)

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

### 6.2.4 Regular Repeat

(in any combination, subject to eligibility conditions)

6.3 All recovery attempts shall be counted within the two-attempt recovery limit. Beyond two (2) attempts a student is required to mandatorily re-register for the failed course in regular mode.

### 6.4 Mandatory Regular Repeat

Where a student has failed a course and exhausted the maximum number of permitted recovery attempts under the Academic Recovery Pathways, Regular Course Repeat shall be the only permissible mechanism to continue attempts to clear the course and shall be mandatory in nature.

The student will re-register for the course and repeat it in regular mode with the subsequent batch. Such re-registration shall require full compliance with attendance, assessment, and academic requirements applicable to the regular offering of the course.
0054.summary: Each course permits 1 regular and 2 recovery attempts via supplementary exam, summer semester, directed reading, or repeat. After exhausting attempts, mandatory regular repeat required.

0055.title: 7. Eligibility Matrix
0055.text:
## 7. Eligibility Matrix

### 7.1 Course-Level Academic Conditions & Recovery Options

### 7.2 Academic Year Back Status

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Academic Year Back | Failure of 50% or more credits in an academic year | Mandatory first registration in all failed / uncleared courses; remaining credit capacity may be used for next academic year courses, | ☑ Eligible to utilise applicable recovery options (Supplementary / Summer / Directed Reading / Regular  |

UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

subject to prerequisites and maximum credit limits* ^{}[] Course Repeat), subject to University rules

* Maximum 27 credits per semester. For BCI/PCI-regulated programmes, where the prescribed semester load exceeds 24 credits, an additional credit overload of up to 15% of the semester's credit requirement may be permitted.

### 7.3 Interpretive Notes

7.3.1 Academic Year Back is the only academic status arising from academic failure and is triggered solely by failure of 50% or more credits in an academic year.

7.3.2 Mandatory backlog-first registration shall apply in all cases and shall take precedence over registration in higher-year courses.

7.3.3 Failure of a prerequisite course blocks registration of subsequent courses until the prerequisite is cleared but does not restrict the recovery pathway used to clear the prerequisite.

7.3.4 Substitution is permitted only for Elective / Exploratory courses and shall not apply to Major Core or prerequisite courses.

7.3.5 All recovery options are subject to eligibility conditions, course availability, seat capacity, and approval by the competent academic authority.
0055.summary: Academic Year Back triggered by ≥50% credit failure; recovery options depend on condition—failed courses with ≥75% attendance get all options, debarred students only Summer/DR/Repeat.

0056.title: 8. Academic Recovery Pathways: Detailed Provisions
0056.text:
## 8. Academic Recovery Pathways: Detailed Provisions

### 8.1 Supplementary Examination

Purpose

First recovery opportunity for students who met attendance requirements but failed academically in a course.

Eligibility

8.1.1 F grade due to academic failure
8.1.2 Minimum 75% attendance
8.1.3 Continuous Assessment (CA) > 0, sufficient to be awarded a pass category after appearing in end term examinations
8.1.4 Recovery attempts limit not exhausted

Rules

8.1.5 Assessment: End-Term component only (30%)
8.1.6 CA and MCE marks are carried forward
8.1.7 Academic standard no lower than regular End-Term
8.1.8 Conducted only in approved cycles
8.1.9 Fee: As notified by the University
8.1.10 Failure moves the student to the next eligible recovery pathway

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

### 8.2 Summer Semester / Summer School

Purpose

An intensive academic term for structured backlog clearance, prerequisite fulfilment, or permitted grade improvement.

Eligibility

8.2.1 Students debarred from the End-Term Examination due to attendance shortage (<75%) and awarded an Ab grade
8.2.2 Failed Supplementary Examination
8.2.3 Also applicable to students seeking to:
8.2.3.1 Improve grades or boost their CGPA
8.2.3.2 Fulfil prerequisites for advanced courses
8.2.3.3 Accelerate their graduation
8.2.3.4 Pursue double majors or minors
8.2.3.5 Earn additional specializations

Rules

8.2.4 Maximum courses per student: two (2) per academic year
8.2.5 Mandatory attendance: 75%
8.2.6 Full assessment: CA + End-Term
8.2.7 Academically intensive and not a lenient alternative
8.2.8 Subject to faculty availability and institutional capacity
8.2.9 Summer credits do not count toward regular semester credit ceiling, i.e. 27 credits per semester

### 8.3 Directed Reading (DR)

Purpose

Faculty-supervised independent learning recovery pathway.

Eligibility

8.3.1 Failed Supplementary Examination
8.3.2 Students debarred from the End-Term Examination due to attendance shortage (<75%) and awarded an F grade

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

Rules

8.3.3 Registration for Directed Reading (DR) courses shall be permitted only up to a maximum of 15% of the total semester credit requirement of the Programme. Where the 15% calculation results in a fractional credit value, the maximum permissible DR credits shall be determined by standard mathematical rounding to the nearest whole credit, with values of 0.50 and above rounded upward and values below 0.50 rounded downward.

8.3.4 Not permitted for labs, studios, internships, dissertations, capstones, or practice-based courses
8.3.5 Assessment: CA + End-Term
8.3.6 Minimum 75% engagement in scheduled sessions
8.3.7 1 contact hour compulsory with the concerned faculty every week
8.3.8 Subject to School approval and faculty availability

### 8.4 Regular Repeat (Full Repeat)

Purpose

Allows full re-registration and completion of a course to clear failure or improve grade where permitted.

Rules

8.4.1 Fresh completion of attendance, CA and End-Term
8.4.2 No carry-forward of earlier CA or Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE)
8.4.3 No additional fee beyond normal semester tuition.

[Note: Where a student is required to repeat a course in regular mode after completion of the minimum prescribed Programme duration (N), such registration shall be treated as extension of Programme duration.

In such cases, the student shall be liable to pay applicable fees for the extended duration, as notified by the University from time to time, in accordance with approved fee regulations.]

8.4.4 No academic relaxation or preferential treatment
8.4.6 Managing timetable clashes and scheduling conflicts are the student's responsibility
8.4.6 Subject to seat availability

### 8.5 Substitution of Elective Courses

Purpose

Provides flexibility for non-core academic components.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

Eligibility

8.5.1 Elective / Exploratory courses only
8.5.2 Not permitted for Major Core or their prerequisite courses
8.5.3 Subject to seat availability

Rules

8.5.4 Elective substitutions are permitted without a fixed cap, provided the approved Programme credit structure and category-wise credit requirements are maintained.
8.5.5 Substituted grade replaces earlier grade
8.5.6 Counts within credit load and attempt limits
8.5.7 No further grade improvement permitted after substitution

### 8.6 Academic Year Back (Recovery & Progression Mode)

Purpose

Mandatory recovery mechanism for significant academic failure within an academic year. Applicable from admission year 2025.

Trigger

8.6.1 Failure of 50% or more registered credits across an academic year

Key Principles

8.6.2 Earned credits remain valid
8.6.3 Backlog clearance takes precedence
8.6.4 Limited forward progression subject to AYB rules
8.6.5 Mandatory academic advising applies

8.7 Operational details are governed by the Academic Year Back & Progression Policy.

0064.title: ACADEMIC YEAR BACK & PROGRESSION POLICY
0064.text:
# ACADEMIC YEAR BACK & PROGRESSION POLICY

(Policy Code:AG-05)

Applicable from Admission Year 2025

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

## 1. Purpose

1.1 This Policy establishes a structured Academic Year Back (AYB) framework at UPES for students who experience significant academic failure within an academic year.

1.2 The Policy operationalises the University's Recovery-First Progression Model, ensuring that students:

1.2.1 Compulsorily prioritise academic recovery before unrestricted progression
1.2.2 Progress in a controlled, prerequisite-protected, and academically viable manner
1.2.3 Remain within UGC-prescribed maximum Programme duration norms (N + 2 + 1)

1.3 The Policy aims to:

1.3.1 Prevent uncontrolled accumulation of academic backlogs
1.3.2 Preserve curriculum sequencing and prerequisite integrity
1.3.3 Provide a transparent, fair, and auditable recovery-cum-progression pathway
1.3.4 Protect students from long-term academic, financial, and regulatory risk

1.4 Programmes governed by statutory or professional councils (such as PCI-regulated or Law Programmes) may be subject to additional or modified progression rules, which shall prevail to the extent of any inconsistency with this Policy.

## 2. Scope

This Policy applies to:

2.1 All full-time Undergraduate and Postgraduate students of UPES
2.2 All Schools, Programmes, and Academic Units
2.3 All credit-bearing courses contributing to Programme completion

## 3. Trigger for Academic Year Back

A student shall be placed on Academic Year Back if:

3.1 The student fails fifty per cent (50%) or more of the total registered credits in an academic year, calculated cumulatively across Odd and Even semesters.

3.1.1 Mandatory Nature

3.1.1.1 Placement on Academic Year Back is mandatory, automatic, and non-discretionary upon meeting the trigger condition.
3.1.1.2 Voluntary or elective placement on Academic Year Back is not permitted.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

## 4. Nature of Academic Year Back

### 4.1 An Academic Year Back:

4.1.1 Does not constitute repetition of the entire academic year
4.1.2 Does not annul or invalidate earned credits
4.1.3 Operates as a **structured recovery-and-progression year**, not a punitive measure

### 4.2 The Academic Year Back framework is **corrective in intent**, designed to prioritise recovery while safeguarding long-term academic progression and regulatory compliance.

## 5. Academic Registration Framework During Academic Year Back

### 5.1 Mandatory Backlog Clearance

During Academic Year Back, the student must compulsorily register for all failed courses, as applicable:

5.1.1 Failed Odd Semester courses → registered in the next Odd Semester
5.1.2 Failed Even Semester courses → registered in the next Even Semester
5.1.2 Where a backlog course is offered through **Supplementary Examination** or **Directed Reading (DR)**, registration may occur in the semester/term in which such mode is officially notified and permitted.

The Odd-Odd and Even-Even registration principle shall primarily apply when the course is being repeated in Regular Mode.

Key principles:

5.1.3 Backlog clearance shall take absolute precedence over all other registrations
5.1.4 No student on Academic Year Back may defer backlog registration in favour of new courses
5.1.5 All registrations remain subject to course-level attempt limits
5.1.6 Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP) remains available for students to clear backlogs
5.1.7 **Directed Reading (DR)**:

Directed Reading (DR) shall be permitted up to a **maximum of 15%** of the total registered credits of the semester.

Any fractional value resulting from the 15% calculation shall be rounded to the nearest whole credit, with values of 0.5 and above rounded up, and values below 0.5 rounded down.

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

### 5.2 Limited Forward Progression

(Up to 50% of Normal Annual Credit Load)

In addition to mandatory backlog courses, a student on Academic Year Back may be permitted to register for new courses up to a maximum of the remaining percentage of the normal annual credit load, subject to:

- 5.2.1 No timetable clashes
- 5.2.2 All prerequisites being fully cleared
- 5.2.3 Adherence to semester-wise credit limits
- 5.2.4 Explicit approval through the Individual Study Plan (ISP)

There shall be no entitlement to register for new courses beyond this limit.

### 5.3 Clarificatory Credit Examples (Academic Year Back)

|  Example | Normal Annual Credit Load | Credits Failed | % Failed | Mandatory Backlog Registration | Maximum New Credits Allowed (Next Academic Year)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Example 1 | 40 credits | 20 credits | 50% | All 20 failed credits | Up to 20 credits (50%)  |
|  Example 2 | 40 credits | 24 credits | 60% | All 24 failed credits | Up to 16 credits (40%)  |
|  Example 3 | 44 credits | 22 credits | 50% | All 22 failed credits | Up to 22 credits (50%)  |

**Principle:**
Forward progression is always limited to the remaining feasible percentage, such that the total academic load remains realistic, recoverable, and academically sound.

### 5.4 Advanced Course Registration (Conditional)

Where:

- 5.4.1 Timetable feasibility exists, and
- 5.4.2 All prerequisites are fully satisfied,

A student may be permitted to register for advanced-level courses only as part of the approved Individual Study Plan.

Such registration:

- 5.4.3 Requires explicit academic approval

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

5.4.4 Shall not be construed as an entitlement
5.4.5 Remains subject to revision or withdrawal based on academic performance

## 6. Mandatory Academic Advising & Individual Study Plan (ISP)

### 6.1 Advising Requirement

Every student placed on Academic Year Back shall undergo mandatory academic advising with the Programme Lead / Cluster Head.

The advising process shall review:

- 6.1.1 Failed courses and recovery attempt status
- 6.1.2 Prerequisite blocks and sequencing constraints
- 6.1.3 Credit feasibility per semester
- 6.1.4 Impact on expected graduation timeline

### 6.2 Individual Study Plan (ISP)

An Individual Study Plan (ISP) shall be created for each Academic Year Back student, specifying:

- 6.2.1 Backlog courses to be cleared semester-wise
- 6.2.2 New courses (if any) approved for registration

#### 6.2.3 Semester-wise credit load

- 6.2.4 Impact on graduation timeline and Programme duration

The ISP shall be:

- 6.2.5 Approved and signed by the Programme Lead / Cluster Head
- 6.2.6 Submitted to the Academic Planning & Operations (APO)
- 6.2.7 Used for controlled, manual registration in the ERP

No deviation from the approved ISP shall be permitted without Dean-level approval.

## 7. Student-Parent / Guardian Undertaking

As a mandatory condition for registration under Academic Year Back:

7.1 The student and parent / guardian shall jointly sign an Undertaking, acknowledging:

- 7.1.1 Possible delay in graduation
- 7.1.2 Additional financial implications (including Summer Semester, Directed Reading, or extended duration) *

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

7.1.3 Possibility of Increased academic workload and reduced flexibility
7.1.4 Non-entitlement to concessions beyond this Policy

7.2 No registration under Academic Year Back shall be processed without the signed Undertaking.

* Regular course repeat beyond the prescribed Programme duration (N) shall be subject to extended-duration fees, as notified by the University.

## 8. Academic Rigour & Progression Principles

The Academic Year Back framework operates on the following non-negotiable principles:

8.1 Academic backlogs must be cleared before full progression
8.2 Recovery and progression pathways are more rigorous than regular progression
8.3 Forward movement is conditional, capped, and closely monitored

## 9. Governance & Responsibilities

|  Stakeholder | Responsibility  |
| --- | --- |
|  Programme Lead / Cluster Head | Academic counselling, ISP approval  |
|  Academic Planning & Operations (APO) | ERP control, manual registration  |
|  Dean | Academic oversight  |
|  Student | Compliance with ISP and policy  |
|  Parent / Guardian | Acknowledgement of implications  |

## 10. Records, Audit & Compliance

10.1 All Academic Year Back cases shall be recorded in the ERP
10.2 Records shall be retained for a minimum of five (5) years
10.3 Deviations from approved ISP require Dean-level approval
10.4 This Policy operates strictly within UGC maximum Programme duration norms (N + 2 + 1)

## 11. Review, Interpretation & Amendment

This Policy shall be:

11.1 Reviewed annually by the Academic Council
11.2 Amended as required to align with regulatory or institutional changes

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

The University reserves the right to interpret and implement this Policy in alignment with approved academic frameworks and regulatory requirements.

All interpretations issued by the University shall be final and binding.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0064.summary: This policy mandates academic recovery before progression for students with significant academic failure, limiting total programme duration to N+2+1 years under UGC norms.

0065.title: SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATION POLICY
0065.text:
# SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATION POLICY

(Policy Code: AG-06)

This Policy shall come into force with immediate effect.

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
UNIVERSITY OF SYDNEY

## 1. Purpose

1.1 The Supplementary Examination Policy establishes a structured, time-bound academic recovery mechanism for students who:

1.1.1 Have failed a course due to academic reasons, or
1.1.2 Were eligible but unable to appear in the End-Term Examination for valid academic reasons, or
1.1.3 Have passed a course and are seeking grade improvement, where permitted,

1.2 While ensuring academic rigour, transparency, fairness, auditability, and timely academic progression.
1.3 This Policy operates within the framework of the UPES Academic Progression Framework and Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP)

## 2. Scope

2.1 This Policy applies to:

2.1.1 All full-time Undergraduate and Postgraduate students of UPES
2.1.2 All Schools and Programmes
2.1.3 Only those courses for which Supplementary Examination windows are formally notified by the Controller of Examinations (CoE)

## 3. Definitions

|  Term | Definition  |
| --- | --- |
|  Supplementary Examination | A re-examination opportunity for eligible students to clear a course by reappearing in the End-Term Examination component.  |
|  Improvement Examination | Use of the Supplementary Examination mechanism by eligible students to improve a previously awarded passing grade, governed by the Grade Improvement Policy.  |
|  Debarred | A student barred from the End-Term Examination due to attendance below 75% or other eligibility restrictions.  |
|  Theory Course | A course assessed through a written End-Term Examination.  |
|  Non-Theory Course | Courses assessed through labs, projects, studios, internships, dissertations, jury-based or practice-based evaluation.  |

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

## 4. Eligibility Criteria

### 4.1 Supplementary Examination -

A student may register for a Supplementary Examination only if all the following conditions are met:

4.1.1 The student has been awarded an F grade, and
4.1.2 The student was not debarred from the End-Term Examination, and
4.1.3 One of the following applies:

4.1.3.1 Failed the End-Term Examination due to academic reasons, or
4.1.3.2 Was eligible but could not appear in the End-Term Examination, and

4.1.4 Minimum 75% attendance requirement was satisfied, and
4.1.5 Continuous Assessment (CA) > 0, and CA is mathematically sufficient to clear the course after End-Term evaluation, and
4.1.6 The student is within the permitted recovery attempt limits under the Academic Progression Framework.

### 4.2 Cases Where Supplementary Examination Is NOT Permitted

|  Case | Applicable Pathway  |
| --- | --- |
|  Debarred due to attendance (<75%) | Summer Semester / Directed Reading / Regular Repeat  |
|  CA = 0 or CA too low to mathematically clear | Summer Semester / Directed Reading / Regular Repeat  |
|  Academic misconduct penalty | Governed by Academic Integrity Policy  |

### 4.3 Supplementary Examination for Grade Improvement

Students who have successfully passed a course may use the Supplementary Examination for grade improvement, subject to the following:

4.3.1 The student must be eligible under the UPES Grade Improvement Policy
4.3.2 The student must be currently enrolled and academically active
4.3.3 All conditions, limits, transcript treatment, and CGPA implications shall be governed exclusively by the Grade Improvement Policy

4.4 Supplementary Examination does not create an independent entitlement for grade improvement beyond what is permitted under the Grade Improvement Policy.

## 5. Assessment Scope & Examination Structure

5.1 Supplementary / Improvement Examination evaluates only the End-Term component
5.2 Continuous Assessment (CA / IA / MCE) marks cannot be improved
5.3 The Supplementary End-Term score replaces the earlier End-Term score
5.4 No grade capping shall be applied
5.5 Best valid academic performance shall be awarded

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

### 5.6 Duration of Supplementary Examination: as notified by CoE

## 6. Weightage Rules Based on Original Course Attempt Period

|  Original Course Attempt | Supplementary Evaluation Model | Remarks  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  Before July 2023 | 100% Supplementary Exam | Single exam determines result  |
|  July 2023 onwards | End-Term component only (30%) | CA & MCE carried forward  |

Note:
If CA / MCE marks are mathematically insufficient to clear the course, Supplementary registration shall not be permitted, and the student must opt for Summer Semester / Directed Reading / Regular Repeat.

## 7. Attempt Limits & Academic Progression Alignment

7.1 Supplementary Examination constitutes one recovery attempt under the Academic Recovery Pathways.
7.2 A student is permitted a maximum of two (2) recovery attempts per course, through any combination of:

7.2.1 Supplementary Examination
7.2.2 Summer Semester
7.2.3 Directed Reading (DR)
7.2.4 Regular Course Repeat

7.3 Where a student has failed a course and exhausted the maximum number of permitted recovery attempts under the Academic Recovery Pathways, Regular Course Repeat shall be the only permissible mechanism to continue attempts to clear the course and shall be mandatory in nature.

## 8. Supplementary Examination Cycles

|  Cycle | Period | Applicability  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  Cycle 1 | July | Combined Odd & Even Semester Courses  |
|  Cycle 2 | Special Cycle | Graduating Batch only  |

## 9. Registration & Administration

|  Item | Provision  |
| --- | --- |
|  Registration Mode | Online via Student Portal / ERP  |
|  Eligibility Validation | System + CoE verification  |

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

|  Registration Window | Opens post result declaration  |
| --- | --- |
|  Fee | ₹2,000 per course (subject to University revision)  |
|  Refund / Withdrawal | Not permitted  |
|  Admit Card | Mandatory for exam entry  |

## 10. Result Processing & Moderation

10.1 Supplementary / Improvement results shall be processed and declared by the CoE
10.2 Results shall undergo moderation as per University Moderation Policy
10.3 Final approval shall rest with the University Result Board
10.4 Transcript notation shall reflect the attempt in a neutral, non-stigmatic manner, as per University policy

## 11. Student Responsibilities & Advisory

11.1 Students shall:

11.1.1 Track official notifications, eligibility criteria, and deadlines
11.1.2 Verify mathematical feasibility of clearance before registration
11.1.3 Seek guidance from Academic Mentor / Course Coordinator / Exam Helpdesk where unsure

11.2 Important Advisory:

Applying for or appearing in a Supplementary Examination does not guarantee eligibility. Incorrect or ineligible registration shall still be counted as a recovery attempt and shall be non-refundable.

11.3 All Supplementary Examinations are conducted offline, and physical presence is mandatory.

## 12. Governance & Accountability

## Revision & Discretion Clause

The University reserves the right to revise fees, examination rules, eligibility conditions, timelines, and operational modalities related to Supplementary Examinations in accordance with regulatory requirements, academic considerations, or administrative exigencies. All revisions shall be notified through official University communication channels and shall be binding.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0065.summary: Full-time UPES Undergraduates and Postgraduates may clear failed courses or improve grades via officially notified Supplementary Examinations under the Academic Recovery Framework.

0066.title: DIRECTED READING (DR) POLICY
0066.text:
# DIRECTED READING (DR) POLICY

(Policy Code: AG-07)

This Policy shall come into force with immediate effect.

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

## 1. Purpose

1.1 Directed Reading (DR) is a **structured, faculty-supervised academic recovery mechanism** that enables eligible students to complete a failed or debarred course through a personalised learning plan, in lieu of regular classroom-based delivery.

1.2 Directed Reading:

1.2.1 Operates as part of UPES’s **Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP)**

1.2.2 Is designed to ensure **no dilution of academic standards**, with **structured and closely monitored engagement**, and **learning outcomes exceeding those of regular course delivery**.

1.3 Directed Reading is **not an entitlement** and shall be permitted only through a defined academic approval process.

## 2. Scope

2.1 This policy applies to:

2.1.1 All full-time **Undergraduate and Postgraduate** students of UPES

2.1.2 Eligible courses approved for Directed Reading in each academic cycle

2.2 DR offerings shall be limited to courses approved based on **academic feasibility, faculty availability, and recovery need**, and shall not operate as a parallel or substitute delivery mode.

## 3. Definitions

|  Term | Definition  |
| --- | --- |
|  Directed Reading (DR) | A supervised academic engagement where a student completes a course through an approved learning plan, weekly faculty interaction, and formal assessment  |
|  Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP) | Institutionally approved mechanisms for academic recovery, including Supplementary Examination, Summer Semester, Directed Reading, Regular Course Repeat, Substitution and Academic Year Back.  |
|  Debarred Student | A student not permitted to appear for the End-Term Examination due to attendance below 75%  |
|  Academic Failure | A student who failed a course academically despite meeting attendance eligibility (≥75%)  |

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY, 1900

## 4. Eligibility for Directed Reading

### 4.1 Academic Failure (Attendance ≥75%)

A student may opt for Directed Reading if the student:

- 4.1.1 Has received an Ab grade with attendance ≥75%, or
- 4.1.2 Has failed the Supplementary Examination

### 4.2 Debarred Students (Attendance <75%)

A student who has been debarred due to attendance shortage (<75%) and awarded an F (Debarred) grade:

- 4.2.1 May opt for either Directed Reading OR Summer Semester to clear the debarred course
- 4.2.2 Is not eligible for Supplementary Examination for the debarred course
- 4.2.3 Must clear the debarred course before registering for any course where it is a prerequisite

The choice between Directed Reading and Summer Semester shall be exercised by the student, subject to academic feasibility and availability.

### 4.3 Attempt Limits and Academic Standing

Directed Reading may be permitted only after a student has made at least one regular attempt in the course, where a regular attempt means formal registration for the course in a regular semester with full teaching-learning and assessment exposure, irrespective of the final grade outcome.

Directed Reading shall constitute one (1) recovery attempt under the Academic Recovery Pathways.

A maximum of three (3) total attempts per course is permitted across the Programme, inclusive of:

- 4.3.1 Regular Semester (first attempt)
- 4.3.2 Any two (2) recovery attempts through a combination of:
- 4.3.2.1 Supplementary Examination
- 4.3.2.2 Summer Semester
- 4.3.2.3 Directed Reading
- 4.3.2.4 Regular Course Repeat
- 4.3.2.5 Substitution of an Elective/Optional course to clear a 'F' grade

Where a student has failed a course and exhausted the maximum number of permitted recovery attempts under the Academic Recovery Pathways, Regular

Course Repeat shall be the only permissible mechanism to continue attempts to clear the course and shall be mandatory in nature.

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

5. Restrictions and Limits

|  Parameter | Rule  |
| --- | --- |
|  Maximum DR per student | Registration for Directed Reading (DR) courses shall be permitted only up to a maximum of 15% of the total current semester credit requirement of the Programme. Where the 15% calculation results in a fractional credit value, the maximum permissible DR credits shall be determined by standard mathematical rounding to the nearest whole credit, with values of 0.50 and above rounded upward and values below 0.50 rounded downward.  |
|  Faculty supervision limit | 2 DR course per faculty per cycle  |
|  Eligible course types | Theory or Theory + Lab (where lab outcomes can be mapped to assignments)  |
|  Not permitted for | Pure labs, studios, workshops, internships, capstones, dissertations  |
|  Engagement requirement | Minimum 75% structured academic engagement  |
|  Failure in DR | Failure in Directed Reading shall be treated as exhaustion of one recovery attempt.
Further recovery options, if any, shall be governed by the remaining attempt eligibility under the Academic Progression Framework. Where recovery attempts are exhausted, the student shall be governed by repeat provisions as applicable.  |

6. Assessment Structure

6.1 Directed Reading follows fresh evaluation, aligned with regular semester standards.

|  Component | Weightage  |
| --- | --- |
|  Continuous Assessment (Assignments /Mid-Course Evaluations) | 70%  |
|  End-Term Examination / Viva / Final Evaluation | 30%  |

6.2 Assessment Rules:

6.2.1 The assessment weightage for Directed Reading shall follow a 70:30 structure
6.2.2 No internal or midcourse evaluation marks from previous attempts shall be carried forward

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

6.2.3 Assessments must map to Course Outcomes (COs)
6.2.4 The assessment plan must be approved by the School Cluster Head
6.2.5 End-Term evaluation shall be conducted in the next available examination cycle as notified by the Controller of Examinations
6.2.6 Assessments should be more rigorous than the regular course

7. Fees
7.1 ₹3,000 per credit
7.2 Full fee payment is mandatory at the time of registration

Refunds
7.3 Refunds shall be permitted only in cases of:
7.3.1 University-led cancellation of DR approval
7.3.2 Verified administrative or payment error
7.4 No refund shall be permitted for voluntary withdrawal by the student.

8. Documentation and Records
All DR-related records—including applications, learning plans, attendance logs, assessments, and results—shall be:
8.1 Maintained in ERP / LMS
8.2 Retained for a minimum of five (5) years
8.3 Subject to audit and quality review

9. Communication
The University shall notify Directed Reading application timelines, procedures, and eligibility conditions at the beginning of each semester through official University communication channels. Communication on DR application process to initiate at the beginning of the semester by office of the VC/ Pro-VC/ Vice Pro-vost.

10. Policy Review
This policy shall be reviewed annually by the Academic Council and updated as required to remain aligned with:
10.1 Academic Recovery Pathways
10.2 Academic Progression Framework
10.3 Regulatory and operational requirements

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY

## 11. Governance & Accountability

The University reserves the right to revise fees, examination rules, eligibility conditions, timelines, and operational modalities related to Directed Reading at any time, based on regulatory requirements, academic considerations, or administrative exigencies. Such revisions shall be notified through official University communication channels and shall be binding on all students.

January 2026

UPES UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY
0066.summary: DR is a supervised academic recovery for failed/debarred courses, part of ARP, requiring approval to maintain standards, limited to approved courses for UG/PG students.

0067.title: SUMMER SEMESTER POLICY
0067.text:
# SUMMER SEMESTER POLICY

(Policy Code: AG-08)

This Policy shall come into force with immediate effect.

January 2026

UPES
UNIVERSITY OF TOMORROW

## 1. Purpose

1.1 The Summer Semester is an intensive additional academic term designed to support timely academic progression and structured academic recovery, without dilution of academic standards.

1.2 It enables eligible students to:

1.2.1 Clear failed or debarred courses
1.2.2 Recover from academic failure without delaying progression
1.2.3 Fulfil prerequisite requirements for advanced coursework
